questions.question: What does `useActionState(action, initialState)` return, in order?
options: `[state, formAction, isPending]`
`[formAction, state, isPending]`
`[state, dispatch]`
`[isPending, state, formAction]`
questions.explanation: `useActionState` returns `[state, formAction, isPending]`: the current state, the action to wire to a `<form>`, and a boolean that is true while the action runs.

questions.question: An Action passed to `useActionState` has which signature?
options: `(previousState, formData) => newState`
`(formData) => newState`
`(event, formData) => void`
`(formData, previousState) => Promise<void>`
questions.explanation: The action receives `(previousState, formData)` and returns the next state. `previousState` lets you accumulate or return errors without extra `useState`.

questions.question: From which package is `useFormStatus` imported?
options: `react-dom`
`react`
`react-router`
`react-dom/server`
questions.explanation: `useFormStatus` is a react-dom hook (it depends on DOM form semantics). `useActionState` and `useOptimistic`, by contrast, come from `react`.

questions.question: In React 19 you can render a context object directly as the provider, e.g. `<ThemeContext value="dark">`, instead of `<ThemeContext.Provider value="dark">`.
questions.explanation: React 19 lets you render the context directly as the provider. The `.Provider` form still works but is slated for deprecation, with a codemod provided.

questions.question: In React 19, how does a function component most idiomatically receive a `ref`?
options: As an ordinary prop, destructured alongside other props
Only by wrapping the component in `forwardRef`
Via a special `useRef` parameter injected by React
Through `this.refs` on the component instance
questions.explanation: React 19 passes `ref` as an ordinary prop, so `forwardRef` is no longer needed (it still works but is slated for deprecation). Function components have no `this`/instance, ruling out `this.refs`.

questions.question: A developer calls `useFormStatus()` in the same component that renders the `<form>` and finds `pending` is always `false`. Why?
options: `useFormStatus` reads the nearest **parent** `<form>`, so the calling component must be rendered **inside** that form, not the one that renders it
`useFormStatus` only works inside Server Components
`pending` is only `true` for forms that use `useOptimistic`
`useFormStatus` must be passed the form's ref as an argument to track it
questions.explanation: `useFormStatus` reads the status of the nearest parent `<form>`. The component that renders the `<form>` is not inside it, so it always sees `pending: false`; the hook must live in a child like a `SubmitButton`.

questions.question: When an Action using `useOptimistic` fails (the async mutation throws), what happens to the UI?
options: The optimistic value is dropped and the UI re-derives from the unchanged real state, returning to its prior value
React runs a dedicated rollback handler you registered with `useOptimistic`
The optimistic value persists until you manually call a `revert` function
React replays the optimistic update against the server until it succeeds
questions.explanation: There is no failure-specific rollback. On error the parent state was simply never updated, so once the transition settles React drops the transient optimistic value and re-derives from the unchanged real state.

questions.question: Given `const [optimistic, addOptimistic] = useOptimistic(messages, reducer)`, when should `addOptimistic(value)` be called?
options: Inside the Action, **before** awaiting the async mutation
After the async mutation resolves, to commit the value
Inside a `useEffect` that watches `messages`
Only from the `useFormStatus` submit button
questions.explanation: You call `addOptimistic(value)` inside the Action before `await`, so the optimistic value renders instantly while the mutation is in flight; it is dropped once the real state settles.

questions.question: Unlike React hooks, the `use` API may be called conditionally — inside an `if`, inside a loop, or after an early `return`.
questions.explanation: `use` is not bound by the Rules of Hooks ordering constraint, so it can run conditionally — e.g. reading context only when needed, or after an early return.

questions.question: What does `use(promise)` do during render?
options: Suspends the component until the Promise resolves, integrating with the nearest `<Suspense>` boundary (and error boundaries on rejection)
Returns immediately with `undefined`, then re-renders when the Promise settles
Throws synchronously unless wrapped in a `try/catch`
Blocks the JavaScript main thread until the Promise resolves
questions.explanation: `use(promise)` suspends the component and works with the nearest `<Suspense>` boundary for the loading state, and routes rejections to error boundaries. It does not busy-block the thread or return `undefined`.

questions.question: Which fields are part of the object returned by `useFormStatus`? (Select all that apply.)
options: `pending`
`data`
`method`
`isPending`
`state`
questions.explanation: `useFormStatus` returns `{ pending, data, method, action }`. `isPending` is the `useActionState` flag, and `state` is the `useActionState` value — neither belongs to `useFormStatus`.

questions.question: Which of the following were introduced or made first-class in React 19? (Select all that apply.)
options: The `use` API for reading a Promise or context
`useOptimistic`
Rendering `<title>`/`<meta>` anywhere and hoisting them to `<head>`
`useFormState` from react-dom as the recommended form hook
Class component lifecycle methods like `componentDidMount`
questions.explanation: `use`, `useOptimistic`, and document-metadata hoisting are React 19 additions. `ReactDOM.useFormState` is deprecated in favor of `useActionState`, and class lifecycles are long-standing React, not new.

questions.question: How does `useActionState` relate to the older `ReactDOM.useFormState`?
options: It replaces the now-deprecated `useFormState` and additionally surfaces `isPending`
It is an alias for `useFormState` with no behavioral difference
It is a react-dom hook that wraps `useFormState` internally
It is unrelated; `useFormState` remains the recommended API
questions.explanation: `useActionState` (from `react`) replaces the deprecated `ReactDOM.useFormState` and also exposes an `isPending` flag the old hook lacked.

questions.code:
<div ref={(node) => {
  const observer = observe(node);
  return () => observer.disconnect();
}} />
questions.question: In React 19, this ref callback returns a cleanup function. What is the consequence of returning a cleanup?
options: The cleanup runs on unmount, and React **no longer** calls the ref callback again with `null`
React calls the cleanup, then still calls the callback with `null` on unmount as before
Returning a function from a ref callback is ignored; only `useEffect` supports cleanup
The cleanup runs on every re-render, not on unmount
questions.explanation: When a ref callback returns a cleanup function, it runs on unmount (mirroring effect cleanup) and React suppresses the legacy null-call. The old behavior of calling the callback with `null` applies only when you do NOT return a cleanup.

questions.question: In client code, it is fine to create a Promise during render and pass it straight to `use()`.
questions.explanation: A Promise created fresh during render is recreated on every render, breaking Suspense's ability to track it. The Promise must come from a Suspense-aware cache or framework loader before you `use` it.

questions.question: Why do Server Components and Server Actions rarely appear in a plain client-side React 19 app?
options: They require a bundler/server integration that implements the RSC protocol (framework territory like the Next.js App Router)
They were removed from React 19 before stable release
They only work when `forwardRef` is also used
They depend on `useOptimistic`, which is server-only
questions.explanation: Adopting Server Components/Actions is framework territory: you need a bundler and server integration that implements the RSC protocol, so they rarely show up in a plain client-side app.

questions.question: In this pattern, how is a failure surfaced to the component as `error`?
options: By returning an error value from the action (or throwing); the returned value becomes the next `state`
By calling a separate `setError` updater React injects into the action
By rejecting a Promise that React stores in a hidden second slot of the tuple
By writing to `isPending`, which doubles as the error channel
questions.explanation: The action's return value becomes the next `state`, so returning an error value (or throwing) is how failure is reported. Here `error` is just the first tuple element — the latest returned state — not a separate updater or the `isPending` flag.

questions.question: Regarding React 19 metadata and resource features, which statements are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: Rendering `<title>`/`<meta>`/`<link>` anywhere hoists them to `<head>`, so `react-helmet` is no longer needed
`preload`, `preinit`, `prefetchDNS`, and `preconnect` are imported from `react-dom`
`preinit` can load and apply a stylesheet early as a side effect during render
Metadata hoisting requires wrapping tags in a special `<Head>` component from `react`
questions.explanation: React 19 hoists rendered `<title>`/`<meta>`/`<link>` into `<head>` (replacing `react-helmet`), and exposes `preload`/`preinit`/`prefetchDNS`/`preconnect` from `react-dom`; `preinit(..., { as: 'style' })` loads and applies early. There is no required `<Head>` wrapper component.
